title: Regional Credit Union BIN Exploitation with Staged Validation Testing
narrative: Fraudsters identify a small regional credit union's BIN (typically 6-digit range serving <50K cardholders) with limited real-time fraud detection capabilities. Attack begins with 8-15 micro-transactions ($0.50-$2.99) across different merchants to map valid card numbers and identify those without SMS alerts or push notifications. Within 45-90 minutes of successful validation, attackers pivot to 12-25 mid-value purchases ($185-$450) concentrated in digital goods and gift card MCCs. The targeted issuer lacks velocity controls across MCCs and fails to flag the pattern until batch processing 6-18 hours later. Attackers exploit the detection lag window, with 70-85% of fraudulent transactions completing before issuer intervention.
indicators: IIN/BIN belongs to institution with <100K cards issued, often regional credit unions or small banks
Initial phase: 8-15 transactions under $3.00 within 60-minute window, card1_txn_count_1hr between 8-15
Secondary phase: 12-25 transactions $185-$450 within next 90 minutes, card1_amt_zscore >3.5
90%+ transactions in MCC 5816, 5732, 5941, 5968 (digital goods, electronics, gift cards)
Consistent P_emaildomain across attempts (gmail.com, yahoo.com burner accounts)
TransactionAmt clustering: tight ranges $189-$199 or $445-$449 suggesting pre-identified merchant limits
Time gap of 45-90 minutes between micro-validation phase and exploitation phase, detectable via card1_first_txn_delta
summary: Attackers test a small credit union's card numbers with 8-15 tiny purchases under $3 in one hour, then immediately follow with 12-25 larger purchases ($185-$450) for gift cards and digital goods before the issuer's delayed fraud systems catch on.
reasoning: Criminals exploit smaller institutions' slow fraud detection by first validating which stolen card numbers are active and unmonitored, then racing to extract maximum value during the 6-18 hour window before batch processing flags the pattern.
indicator_explanations: Attacking a small-scale issuer with limited fraud controls and detection resources
Testing card validity with tiny purchases before launching the real attack
Ramping up to high-value purchases once the card validates successfully
Targeting convertible-to-cash categories that can be quickly liquidated or resold
Using the same throwaway email repeatedly to coordinate the staged attack
Amounts cluster just under merchant authorization limits to avoid manual review
Deliberate pause between test phase and exploitation suggests coordinated fraud planning
